candidate_problems.text: Can the conjecture be made into a precise, non-metaphorical dynamical criterion for when a branch becomes 'the only strategically stable one'—i.e., a quantitative threshold in terms of redundancy, feedback, irreversibility, and memory that distinguishes mere decoherence from genuinely classical, observer-definite outcomes?

Does the appeal to 'the branch that becomes locally self-reinforcing' actually explain outcome selection, or does it merely redescribe branch-relative definiteness while leaving untouched the central issue of why one experienced outcome occurs rather than all decohered branches persisting as in Everettian accounts?

How, if at all, can this iterated-interaction picture connect to the Born rule: can branch stability, redundancy, or networked environmental witnessing generate the standard quantum probabilities, or is an independent interpretive postulate still unavoidable?
